name: Disk & Filesystem Forensics
overview:
Disk and filesystem forensics involves the acquisition, analysis, and interpretation of data stored on physical and virtual storage media. Investigators examine disk images to recover deleted files, parse filesystem metadata, analyze Windows registry hives, and reconstruct user activity through artifacts like Prefetch files, Shellbags, LNK files, and Jump Lists.

In this learning path, you'll progress from basic filesystem artifact analysis through ransomware investigations, registry forensics, and Active Directory credential extraction. Each challenge builds on previous skills, teaching you to work with real disk images and forensic tools used by incident responders worldwide.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of Windows filesystem concepts (NTFS, FAT32, file paths)
Familiarity with the Windows Registry structure (hives, keys, values)
Basic command-line proficiency (PowerShell or Linux terminal)
A HackTheBox account (free tier is sufficient for most challenges)

coreTools.name: Autopsy
coreTools.description: Open-source GUI forensics platform built on The Sleuth Kit — provides file browsing, keyword search, timeline analysis, and artifact extraction from disk images

installCommands.linux: sudo apt install autopsy / yay -S autopsy

coreTools.name: FTK Imager
coreTools.description: Disk imaging and basic analysis tool — mount images, browse filesystems, calculate hashes, and export files without modifying the source

installCommands.linux: Not natively supported — use a Windows VM
installCommands.macos: Not natively supported — use a Windows VM

coreTools.name: The Sleuth Kit (TSK)
coreTools.description: CLI forensics toolkit — includes fls (file listing), icat (file extraction), mmls (partition layout), fsstat (filesystem details), and more

installCommands.linux: sudo apt install sleuthkit / yay -S sleuthkit

coreTools.name: RegRipper / Registry Explorer
coreTools.description: Windows registry analysis tools — RegRipper extracts forensic artifacts via plugins, Registry Explorer (Eric Zimmerman) provides GUI-based hive browsing

coreTools.name: MFTECmd
coreTools.description: MFT parser from Eric Zimmerman's toolkit — parses $MFT, $J (USN Journal), $LogFile, and $Boot for NTFS forensic analysis

coreTools.name: plaso / log2timeline
coreTools.description: Super timeline generation framework — ingests disk images and produces a unified timeline from all artifact sources (registry, event logs, filesystem timestamps, browser history, etc.)

installCommands.linux: sudo apt install plaso / pip install plaso

coreTools.name: secretsdump.py (Impacket)
coreTools.description: Extracts credentials from ntds.dit (Active Directory database) and SAM/SYSTEM registry hives — essential for AD forensics

entryRecommendation: Start with **Litter** if you're new to disk forensics — it introduces Windows filesystem artifacts like LNK files and Prefetch in a straightforward investigation. If you're comfortable with basic Windows artifacts, jump to **Lockpick** for a ransomware recovery scenario that teaches file carving and encryption analysis.
